dimensions.name: Requirements Met
dimensions.gaps_to_10: Retry button is present (requirement met) but non-functional — after clicking Retry the play button stays permanently disabled because no new Howl instance is created. All other requirements are fulfilled: 3 song tiles ≥120px, cover images with fallback, EN+ZH titles, ≥88px play button, lyric sync with coral highlight, word-chip pause/play/resume, completion banner with Listen Again + Choose Another, play counts persisted in IndexedDB, auto-scroll.

dimensions.name: Error Handling
dimensions.gaps_to_10: The critical audio error recovery path is broken. Clicking 'Retry / 重试' calls `setAudioError(false); setAudioReady(false)` but does NOT re-create the Howl instance. The useEffect that creates Howl has deps `[song.audioPath, handleSongEnd, cancelRaf]` — none change on retry — so the old failed instance remains in howlRef.current. The 'load' event never fires again, audioReady stays false, and the play button is permanently disabled. Users who see the 'Song unavailable' screen and tap Retry will be silently stuck on a player with an inert play button. DB and page-level error handling are solid.

dimensions.name: UX Completeness
dimensions.gaps_to_10: Retry button appears to work (error banner disappears) but leaves the user on a permanently disabled play button — a broken interactive element. Everything else is complete: loading states, bilingual text, song picker empty-state, completion banner, back navigation throughout.

dimensions.name: Code Quality
dimensions.gaps_to_10: SongPlayer.tsx is 235 lines, exceeding the 150-line component limit stated in project rules. The `db as never` cast in SongTime.tsx line ~31 (`putSong(db as never, ...)`) bypasses TypeScript's type system unsafely; the prop should be typed as `IDBPDatabase<LittleBridgeDB> | null` to match db.ts signatures. These are code-style violations but do not cause runtime errors given the surrounding null check.

dimensions.name: Test Coverage
dimensions.gaps_to_10: 16 tests cover all major happy and error paths. The 'retry button on audio error clears error state' test only asserts that the error banner disappears — it does not assert that a new Howl instance was created or that audioReady eventually becomes true, so the P1 bug is invisible to the test suite. An error-path test that verifies the play button becomes enabled (or that Howl constructor was called a second time) after retry would catch this.

dimensions.name: Security
dimensions.gaps_to_10: No hardcoded API keys or secrets. No user-provided text flows into DB queries or LLM prompts. Song/lyric content is static seed data. Minor: coverImagePath from DB is used in next/image src — acceptable since it is app-controlled seed data, not user input.

issues.description: The 'Retry / 重试' button does not actually retry audio loading. It only calls `setAudioError(false); setAudioReady(false)`, which hides the error banner and shows the player UI, but leaves the original failed Howl instance in `howlRef.current`. Because the Howl creation `useEffect` depends only on `[song.audioPath, handleSongEnd, cancelRaf]` — none of which change — no new Howl instance is created. The `load` event never fires, `audioReady` stays false, and the play button's `disabled={!audioReady && !audioError}` condition evaluates to `disabled={true}`. The user is permanently stuck on a player with a greyed-out, non-responsive play button after retrying.
issues.fix_guidance: Introduce a `retryCount` state variable (initially 0) and increment it when the Retry button is clicked: `onClick={() => { setAudioError(false); setAudioReady(false); setRetryCount(c => c + 1); }}`. Add `retryCount` to the Howl creation useEffect's dependency array: `useEffect(() => { ... new Howl(...) ... }, [song.audioPath, handleSongEnd, cancelRaf, retryCount])`. This forces a new Howl instance on each retry, allowing the audio to re-attempt loading. The old Howl instance is cleaned up by the existing `howl.unload()` return function.

issues.description: Component is 235 lines, exceeding the 150-line project rule. The RAF sync loop, word-chip interaction, and completion banner are all inlined. This increases cognitive load for future maintainers.
issues.fix_guidance: Extract the lyric scroll container (lines ~150–195) into a `<LyricScroller>` sub-component that accepts `lyrics`, `currentLineIndex`, `vocabulary`, `activatedWordId`, and `onWordTap` as props. This brings SongPlayer under 150 lines and isolates the lyric rendering concern.

issues.description: `putSong(db as never, ...)` uses an unsafe `as never` cast to bypass TypeScript. The prop `db: IDBPDatabase | null` uses the generic base type instead of the fully-typed `IDBPDatabase<LittleBridgeDB>`, causing the mismatch.
issues.fix_guidance: Import `LittleBridgeDB` (or a re-exported equivalent) from `@/lib/db` and type the prop as `db: IDBPDatabase<LittleBridgeDB> | null`. The `putSong` call then type-checks without any cast.

summary: The Song Time activity is substantially complete: all 3 nursery rhyme tiles render with EN+ZH titles, cover art fallbacks, play-count badges, Howler-driven playback, real-time lyric highlighting, word-chip tap interactions, completion banner, and IndexedDB persistence. All 16 tests pass. However there is one P1 bug: the Retry button after audio load failure does not re-create the Howl instance, leaving users with a permanently-disabled play button after tapping Retry — a broken interactive element that users will encounter on every visit since the MP3 files are not yet generated. The fix is straightforward (add a retryCount dependency to the Howl useEffect). Two P2 issues exist: SongPlayer exceeds the 150-line component limit (235 lines) and there is an unsafe `db as never` type cast. The Error Handling dimension scores 6/10 (< 7), triggering a fail verdict.
